lessons.title: Lesson 1: What Is a Computer Network
lessons.overview: A computer network is a group of devices connected together so they can communicate and share information.
lessons.definition: A computer network is created when two or more devices connect and exchange data. These devices can include computers, phones, printers, or servers. The connection can be made using cables or wireless technologies like Wi-Fi. Networks allow devices to share resources such as files, printers, and internet connections.
examples: Sending a file from one computer to another
Multiple computers using the same printer in an office
Connecting your phone and laptop to the same Wi-Fi router at home
keyPoints: A network connects two or more devices
Devices communicate by sending and receiving data
Networks can use cables or wireless connections

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Why Networks Are Important
lessons.overview: Computer networks make modern digital communication possible.
lessons.definition: Today most digital services depend on networks. When you browse websites, send emails, watch videos, or use online apps, your device communicates with other computers through networks. Businesses use networks to share data and applications. Schools use them for online learning. Banks use them to process financial transactions.
examples: Opening a website sends a request from your device to a web server
Employees sharing files through a company network
keyPoints: They support online services and communication
Most modern systems depend on networks

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Real-World Network Examples
lessons.overview: Networks exist everywhere, from homes to global internet systems.
lessons.definition: Networks can be small or extremely large depending on their purpose. A home network connects devices like phones, laptops, and smart TVs. A company network connects employee computers and servers. The internet is the largest network in the world, connecting billions of devices globally.
examples: A home Wi-Fi network connecting phones, laptops, and smart TVs
An office network used by employees to share files
The internet connecting computers around the world
keyPoints: Networks can be small or very large
Home, office, and internet networks all follow similar principles
Networks allow devices to share resources

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Client and Server
lessons.overview: In many networks, devices have different roles such as client and server.
lessons.definition: A client is a device that requests information or services. Examples include computers, phones, or tablets. A server is a powerful computer that stores data or provides services to clients. When a client sends a request, the server processes it and sends back a response.
examples: Your browser requesting a webpage from a web server
An email application connecting to a mail server
A company computer accessing files stored on a file server
keyPoints: Most internet systems use the client-server model

lessons.title: Lesson 5: How Data Travels on the Internet
lessons.overview: Data travels across networks in small pieces called packets.
lessons.definition: When information is sent through a network, it is divided into smaller units called packets. Each packet contains part of the data along with information about the sender and destination. Network devices such as routers move these packets across networks. When all packets arrive, the receiving device puts them back together to recreate the original message.
examples: Sending a photo online splits the image into many packets
Packets travel through different network paths
The receiving device combines the packets to rebuild the photo
keyPoints: Devices reassemble packets to restore the original data
